To recognize the role that similarity plays in the liking process, let's examine the results of a study of negotiators who had to bargain against one another by email on a difficult business contract. Now email is probably the most bloodless communication channel we have ever devised. There's little in the way of human connection that it allows no chance to meet or see the other person, no chance to get to know them. There are just words that appear on the screen. As a result, the study produced a regrettable number of deadlock negotiations in which no mutually acceptable agreement emerged. In fact, 30% of the time, both sides walked away with nothing. But then, the researchers made a small change in the procedures of the study that changed everything. Another sample of negotiators was asked to send some personal information to their bargaining opponent before the negotiation began. Descriptions of their hobbies, interests, music preferences, family background, that sort of thing. The results were dramatic. The percentage of deadlocked, windless negotiations dropped from 30% to 6%. The most instructive thing for us in the study was that it wasn't the amount of information that the bargainers exchanged that made the difference. Instead it was the number of similarities that appeared inside the information that did it. Oh, you're an only child, so am I. Or you're addicted to Korean barbecue, me too. Or we both love scary movies, how about that? The key factor was the commonalities they shared. Commonalities they didn't know existed until they exchanged personal information with one another. So what's the point of all this for us? If you are negotiating with another party before you start the process, spend the time to identify similarities, even small ones, they'll significantly raise the odds of achieving a mutually desirable outcome.
